name: Granada
summary: Granada is a very safe Andalusian city with low violent crime rates and excellent tourist infrastructure. The main concerns are petty theft in crowded tourist areas like the Alhambra and Albaicin, but violent crime against tourists is rare. Spain maintains a Level 2 travel advisory from the US State Department due to terrorism concerns.
quickVerdict: Granada is one of Spain's safest cities for tourists, consistently ranking among the top destinations for solo travelers and families. With its well-developed infrastructure, friendly locals, and strong police presence, visitors can feel secure exploring this historic gem. While petty theft occurs in tourist hotspots, following basic precautions ensures a worry-free experience. The city's compact size, walkable historic center, and late-night street culture contribute to its overall safety profile.

neighborhoods.name: Centro-Sagrario
neighborhoods.description: The historic city center around Granada Cathedral and Plaza Nueva is well-patrolled and lively throughout the day and evening. Popular tapas areas like Calle Navas are bustling with locals and tourists alike.

neighborhoods.name: Realejo
neighborhoods.description: This charming historic neighborhood below the Alhambra is known for its family-friendly atmosphere, lower crime rates, and regular police patrols. Campo del Principe square is particularly vibrant and secure.

neighborhoods.name: Albaicin
neighborhoods.description: The UNESCO World Heritage Moorish quarter is generally safe during the day with stunning views from Mirador San Nicolas. Exercise extra caution on narrow winding streets at night and be aware of pickpockets in tourist areas.

neighborhoods.name: Cartuja
neighborhoods.description: University area popular with students and young travelers. Well-connected by public transport and maintains good safety standards, especially around the campus areas.

neighborhoods.name: Sacromonte
neighborhoods.description: Famous cave district known for flamenco shows. While culturally significant, some areas can feel isolated at night. Best visited with guided tours, especially for evening flamenco performances.

neighborhoods.name: Chana
neighborhoods.description: Northern residential area with higher crime rates and less tourist-friendly atmosphere. The bus station area requires particular caution, especially at night. Most tourists have no reason to visit this area.

scams.name: Lucky Charm Distraction
scams.description: People approach tourists offering rosemary sprigs or other 'lucky charms' while accomplices pickpocket belongings. Common near Alhambra and Plaza Nueva.
scams.howToAvoid: Politely decline any unsolicited gifts or interactions. Keep bags zipped and in front of you. Say 'no gracias' firmly and keep walking.

scams.name: Petition Signature Scam
scams.description: Individuals with clipboards ask tourists to sign fake petitions for charities while accomplices steal wallets or phones from distracted victims.
scams.howToAvoid: Never sign petitions from strangers on the street. Keep valuables secure and be suspicious of anyone trying to get your attention in crowded areas.

scams.name: Overpriced Tour Guides
scams.description: Unofficial guides near the Alhambra offer tours at inflated prices or provide misleading information, sometimes pressuring tourists to pay upfront.
scams.howToAvoid: Book tours only through official channels, reputable companies, or the Granada Tourism Office. Verify credentials and prices before agreeing to any tour.

scams.name: Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Some taxi drivers may take longer routes, refuse to use the meter, or charge excessive fares, especially from the airport or tourist areas.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official white taxis with green bands, ensure the meter is running, or agree on a fare beforehand. Use ride-sharing apps as alternatives.

soloFemale.overview: Granada is very safe for solo female travelers, with most women reporting feeling secure even when walking alone. The city has a strong street life culture that extends late into the evening, providing natural safety in numbers. Spain ranks 32nd in the Global Peace Index, making it one of the safer European destinations. However, some verbal harassment can occur, particularly in nightlife areas.
tips: Stay in well-lit, populated areas especially at night
Trust your instincts and remove yourself from uncomfortable situations
Consider joining group tours or activities to meet other travelers safely
Keep emergency contacts readily available and inform someone of your plans
Use licensed taxis for late-night transportation rather than walking alone

nightSafety.overview: Granada has a vibrant nightlife scene centered around tapas culture and flamenco shows. The streets remain lively until late with families, students, and tourists creating a safe atmosphere. Most nightlife areas like Calle Elvira and Calle Navas are generally secure with good foot traffic until the early hours.
tips: Stick to main streets and well-lit areas after dark
Watch your drink and never accept drinks from strangers
Use official taxis for late-night transport rather than walking alone
Stay with groups when bar-hopping through different neighborhoods
Be particularly cautious in the narrow streets of Albaicin and Sacromonte at night

transport.metro: Granada has a modern metro/tram system with 26 stations connecting the city center to suburban areas. It's safe and efficient, though it doesn't reach the historic center or most tourist attractions. Single tickets cost around €1.40 with the Credibus card offering better value.
transport.rideshare: Uber and local ride-sharing apps operate safely in Granada. Use official pickup points and verify driver details. Payment by card through the app is recommended for transparency.
transport.taxis: Official white taxis with green bands are safe and regulated. Ensure the meter is running or agree on fares beforehand. From the airport to city center costs approximately €30. Taxis are abundant around tourist areas.
transport.tips: Public buses are safe and cover most areas including the Alhambra (lines C30, C32). Buy a Credibus card for discounted fares. Be aware of pickpockets on crowded buses. Walking is safe during daytime in central areas.

customs: Greet with two kisses on the cheek for friends, handshakes for formal meetings
Dinner is typically served late (9pm or later) and meals are leisurely social affairs
Tapas culture is central - order drinks at the bar and receive free tapas in many establishments
Dress modestly when visiting religious sites like churches and the Alhambra
Tipping 5-10% is appreciated in restaurants but not mandatory as service is often included

health.overview: Spain has excellent healthcare infrastructure with about 4 doctors and 6 nurses per 1,000 inhabitants. Granada has several high-quality hospitals including Hospital Vithas Granada and public healthcare through Seguridad Social. Medical care is readily available and meets international standards.
health.water: Tap water in Granada is safe to drink and sourced from the Sierra Nevada mountains. The water is softer with low mineral content compared to other Spanish cities. While safe, some visitors prefer bottled water due to taste preferences from chlorine treatment.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations are required for Spain. Routine vaccinations should be up to date. Spain follows EU vaccination schedules with high coverage rates exceeding 95% for standard vaccines.
health.altitude: Granada sits at 2,400 feet above sea level which may cause mild altitude effects for some visitors. The climate is continental with hot summers (can exceed 104°F) and cold winters. Stay hydrated and use sun protection, especially in summer months.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Madrid: +34 91 587 2200. Consular services available for US citizens. Granada Tourism Office: +34 958 575 202 for local assistance and information.

faq.a: Yes, Granada is very safe for tourists with low violent crime rates. It consistently ranks among Spain's safest cities. The main concerns are petty theft in crowded areas, which can be avoided with basic precautions like securing valuables and staying aware of surroundings.

Granada is generally safe at night with lively streets and good foot traffic until late hours. The tapas culture and nightlife scene keep main areas populated. However, use caution in narrow Albaicin streets and always use licensed taxis for late-night transport.

faq.q: Is Granada safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Granada is very safe for solo female travelers, with many women reporting feeling secure walking alone. The city's social culture and street life provide natural safety. Stay in well-lit areas at night and trust your instincts if situations feel uncomfortable.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Granada?
faq.a: The Chana neighborhood in the north has higher crime rates and should be avoided, especially at night. Exercise extra caution around the bus station area. While Sacromonte is culturally important, some cave areas can feel isolated - visit with tours, especially in the evening.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Granada?
faq.a: Yes, Granada's public transport is safe and reliable. The metro/tram system and city buses are well-maintained with good security. Watch for pickpockets on crowded buses, especially tourist routes to the Alhambra. Taxis are safe when using official white cabs with green bands.
